news_summary: Orangutan mothers demonstrate social intelligence by organizing playdates for their young, highlighting nurturing and cooperative behavior in the animal kingdom.
news_summary_fr: Les mères orangs-outans font preuve d'intelligence sociale en organisant des rendez-vous ludiques pour leurs petits, ce qui met en évidence les comportements protecteurs et coopératifs présents dans le règne animal.
news_summary_es: Las madres orangután demuestran inteligencia social al organizar quedadas para que sus crías jueguen juntas, lo que pone de relieve el comportamiento afectuoso y cooperativo en el reino animal.

articles.title: Orangutan mothers seem to plan playdates for their offspring
articles.summary: Female orangutans are generally solitary, but they travel more and eat less in an apparent effort to ensure their offspring have someone to play with
articles.content:
Female orangutans are generally solitary, but they travel more and eat less in an apparent effort to ensure their offspring have someone to play with

Female orangutans usually raise a single infant at a time ANDREY GUDKOV/Alamy

Orangutan mothers seem to make trips into the territories of other mothers with similarly aged offspring so the youngsters can play together.

Play is essential to how many animals learn, strengthening social and motor skills, and teaching crucial behaviour. Yet orangutans are a solitary species, and mothers give birth to a single infant, which they raise alone for six to seven years. The young socialise together when they get the chance, but how often this happens and how it comes about are poorly understood.

“I think the assumption would be that orangutans require less play because they’re less social than the other apes, but orangutan males have to fight, so they have to practise that somewhere,” says Zarin Machanda at Tufts University in Massachusetts.

To dig into this, Odd Jacobson at the Max Planck Institute of Animal Behavior in Konstanz, Germany, and his colleagues have looked at 15 years’ worth of data on 31 wild Bornean orangutan (Pongo pygmaeus wurmbii) mother-offspring pairs. The data covers about 30,000 hours of observations that reveal where the animals were, whom they were with and what they were doing.

The team found that mothers with similarly aged offspring spent a disproportionately high amount of time together in the same area. Youngsters tended to play in these situations, and play was more likely if the mothers were closely related.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Earth Edition Unmissable news about our planet, delivered straight to your inbox each month. Sign up to newsletter

The distance the apes travelled increased in the days before and after the playdate, as the mothers headed into the neighbour’s territory and then home afterwards.

“Our study provides strong evidence that wild Bornean orangutan mothers adjust their ranging behavior to increase their offspring’s access to social play,” write Jacobson and his colleagues, who didn’t respond to New Scientist’s request for comment.

It is possible the young apes are just playing where orangutans have gathered near a plentiful supply of fruit, much like brown bear cubs have been seen playing together when their mothers converge on a river full of salmon. But the meet-ups happened regardless of how much fruit was available in the area, and the researchers found that the increased travelling meant less time feeding. This led them to suggest that these encounters are planned at a cost to the mothers’ own foraging, rather than being part of it.

It is almost impossible to determine intentionality with behavioural data like this, says Machanda, but she says “it’s possible that there’s something different about the way that mothers play with their babies and peers play, and they’re making a choice to get their babies socialised with peers”.

Adriano Lameira at the University of Warwick, UK, says the findings are in line with what we know about the huge investment orangutans make in raising their offspring and their cognitive capabilities.

He doesn’t think orangutan mothers call ahead to arrange playdates, though. Male orangutans use long calls to communicate their travel direction a day in advance, he says, but females aren’t thought to use long-distance calls for social coordination.

He thinks the meet-ups come down to what the orangutans can hear and see from the trees, their local knowledge – such as which trees are fruiting or where there are big lianas for youngsters to climb – and their ability to understand what others would do.

“One mother will likely be able to estimate, based on the other mother’s last location and typical range, which resources they will be searching for and where they will most likely be,” says Lameira.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a meaningful scientific discovery about orangutan maternal behavior that reveals intentional social planning to benefit offspring development. This insight into animal cognition and social behavior has broad implications for understanding primate intelligence and conservation efforts. The study is based on extensive data and provides detailed context, making it a substantive and inspiring advancement in animal behavior research.
articles.personality_title: Orangutan mothers travel to arrange playdates for their young
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Orangutans are usually solitary animals. Female orangutans raise one baby at a time and spend many years caring for it alone. Playtime with other young orangutans helps develop important skills, but it was unclear how often these playtimes happen.

**What happened** – Scientists studied 31 mother-orangutan pairs in the wild for 15 years, watching where they went and who they met. They found that mothers with babies of similar age often traveled into each other’s territories so their young could play together. These trips meant the mothers traveled more and ate less, showing they made an effort to arrange these meetings.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that orangutan mothers may plan social play opportunities for their babies, even though orangutans are not very social animals. It suggests orangutans have more complex social behavior and thinking than previously known. These findings help us understand how orangutans raise their young and how intelligent they are.

**What's next step** – Researchers will likely continue studying how orangutans communicate and plan their movements. Understanding these behaviors can improve conservation strategies and deepen our knowledge of animal intelligence.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Orangutan mothers change their travel plans to help their babies meet and play with others, showing careful social planning in a mostly solitary species.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les mères orang-outans voyagent pour organiser des rendez-vous de jeu pour leurs petits
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les orangs-outans sont généralement des animaux solitaires. Les femelles élèvent un seul bébé à la fois et s’en occupent seules pendant de nombreuses années. Le jeu avec d’autres jeunes orangs-outans aide à développer des compétences importantes, mais on ne savait pas combien de fois ces jeux avaient lieu.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont étudié 31 mères orangs-outans sauvages pendant 15 ans, observant leurs déplacements et leurs rencontres. Ils ont découvert que les mères avec des petits du même âge se déplaçaient souvent dans le territoire des autres pour que leurs jeunes puissent jouer ensemble. Ces déplacements signifiaient que les mères voyageaient plus et mangeaient moins, montrant qu’elles faisaient un effort pour organiser ces rencontres.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les mères orangs-outans pourraient planifier les occasions de jeu social pour leurs bébés, même si les orangs-outans sont peu sociaux. Cela suggère que ces animaux ont un comportement social et une pensée plus complexes qu’on ne le pensait avant. Ces résultats aident à mieux comprendre comment les orangs-outans élèvent leurs petits et leur intelligence.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement continuer à étudier comment les orangs-outans communiquent et planifient leurs déplacements. Comprendre ces comportements peut améliorer les stratégies de conservation et approfondir nos connaissances sur l’intelligence animale.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les mères orangs-outans modifient leurs déplacements pour que leurs bébés rencontrent et jouent avec d’autres, montrant une planification sociale attentive chez une espèce généralement solitaire.
articles.personality_title_es: Las madres orangutanes viajan para organizar citas de juego para sus crías
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los orangutanes suelen ser animales solitarios. Las hembras crían a una sola cría a la vez y la cuidan solas durante muchos años. Jugar con otros jóvenes ayuda a desarrollar habilidades importantes, pero no se sabía con qué frecuencia ocurrían estos juegos.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos estudiaron a 31 madres orangutanes en la naturaleza durante 15 años, observando sus movimientos y con quién se encontraban. Descubrieron que las madres con crías de edad similar a menudo viajaban a los territorios de otras para que sus pequeños pudieran jugar juntos. Estos viajes hacían que las madres se desplazaran más y comieran menos, mostrando que hacían un esfuerzo para organizar estos encuentros.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que las madres orangutanes pueden planear oportunidades de juego social para sus crías, aunque los orangutanes no sean animales muy sociales. Esto sugiere que tienen un comportamiento social y pensamiento más complejo de lo que se creía. Estos hallazgos ayudan a entender mejor cómo crían a sus crías y su inteligencia.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente seguirán estudiando cómo los orangutanes se comunican y planifican sus movimientos. Entender estos comportamientos puede mejorar las estrategias de conservación y aumentar nuestro conocimiento sobre la inteligencia animal.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las madres orangutanes cambian sus planes de viaje para que sus crías se encuentren y jueguen con otras, mostrando planificación social en una especie mayormente solitaria.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two mother orangutans gently guiding their playful young offspring as they climb and interact together amidst a lush jungle canopy, with subtle hints of neighboring territories visible in the background through intertwining lianas and fruiting trees, rendered in soft, natural earth tones.
